Thinking:
unknown {}

# Goal I am modding out a pinephone. I will use this pinephone as my main programming device for a long hike. I recently realized that the phone was running 100% off my SD card. The phone has 32Gb of EMMC but is not using any of it Here's the output of 'df'. You can see that there is one device, mmcblk0p2, mounted at root. ```bash pine64-pinephone:~/repos/trail/slim$ df -h / Filesystem Size Used Avail Use% Mounted on /dev/mmcblk0p2 29G 9.7G 18G 36% / ``` And here's the output of lsblk. You can see there are two ~32Gb devices: mmcblk0 (the SD card) and mmcblk2 (the EMMC). The reason I think mmcblk2 is EMMC is because it has a few 'hardcoded' boot partitions (mmcblk2boot0, mmcblk2boot1) that would not exist on the SD card. ```bash pine64-pinephone:~/repos/trail/slim$ lsblk NAME MAJ:MIN RM SIZE RO TYPE MOUNTPOINTS mmcblk0 179:0 0 29.1G 0 disk ├─mmcblk0p1 179:1 0 243M 0 part /boot └─mmcblk0p2 179:2 0 28.9G 0 part / mmcblk2 179:32 0 28.9G 0 disk ├─mmcblk2p1 179:33 0 243M 0 part /mnt/emmcboot └─mmcblk2p2 179:34 0 28.7G 0 part /mnt/emmcroot mmcblk2boot0 179:64 0 4M 1 disk mmcblk2boot1 179:96 0 4M 1 disk zram0 253:0 0 512M 0 disk [SWAP] zram1 253:1 0 2G 0 disk ``` At first, I tried to copy the files from mmcblk0p1 -> mmcblk2p1 and mmcblk0p2 -> mmcblk2p2 directly using `dd`. That looked something like: ```bash sudo dd if=/dev/mmcblk0 or=/dev/mmcblk2 bs=4M status=progress conv=sync,noerror ``` However, when I tried to validate the EMMC partitions I hit an error! Theproblem is that the SD card's root partition is _slightly_ larger than EMMC. When we use 'dd' to copy the file system literally it copies the number of blocks the file system supports. This causes an error since the number of blocks that can be supported on the EMMC is slightly less than what can be supported on the SD card. The option Claude gave me at this point was to downsize the file system on the SD card. This bugged me for a couple reasons though: * It's cumbersome: I don't want to have to downsize the SD card root partition, `dd` copy, then upsize again. * It's annoying. I can't downsize the partition "live" on my pinphone. Instead, I have to take the SD card out of the phone, load it into a machine that has an SD reader, then presumably resize with something like GParted. * ^ because of these it also seems a bit risky. what if I screw up? I do not want to lose progress I told Claude this, and it gave me an alternative solution. Instead of using `dd` to do a block-level copy, we can (a) add file systems to the EMMC partitions directly using `mkfs`, (b) mount those file systems at /mnt/emmcboot, /mnt/emmcroot, and (c) use rsync to copy files over. The whole setup looks roughly like this: ## Set up the boot partition ```bash # use vfat file system for boot partition sudo mkfs.vfat -F32 /dev/mmcblk2p1 # mount that partition to /mnt/emmcboot sudo mkdir -p /mnt/emmcboot sudo mount /dev/mmcblk2p1 /mnt/emmcboot # rsync files from our local system (using SD card) to mounted boot partition sudo rsync -aH --numeric-ids --progress --no-perms --no-owner --no-group /boot/ /mnt/emmcboot/ ``` ## Set up the root partition ```bash # use vfat file system for root partition sudo mkfs.ext4 -F32 /dev/mmcblk2p2 # mount that partition to /mnt/emmcroot sudo mkdir -p /mnt/emmcroot sudo mount /dev/mmcblk2p2 /mnt/emmcroot # rsync files from our local system (using SD card) to mounted boot partition sudo rsync -aHAX --numeric-ids --progress --exclude=/mnt --exclude=/dev --exclude=/tmp --exclude=/proc --exclude=/sys --exclude=/run / /mnt/emmcroot ``` ## Verification ```bash pine64-pinephone:~/repos/trail/slim$ # unmount the partitions pine64-pinephone:~/repos/trail/slim$ sudo umount /mnt/emmcroot pine64-pinephone:~/repos/trail/slim$ sudo umount /mnt/emmcboot pine64-pinephone:~/repos/trail/slim$ # run file system consistency checks pine64-pinephone:~/repos/trail/slim$ sudo fsck.ext4 /dev/mmcblk2p2 e2fsck 1.47.3 (8-Jul-2025) /dev/mmcblk2p2: clean, 184054/1880480 files, 2692856/7515136 blocks pine64-pinephone:~/repos/trail/slim$ su do fsck.vfat /dev/mmcblk2p1 fsck.fat 4.2 (2021-01-31) There are differences between boot sector and its backup. This is mostly harmless. Differences: (offset:original/backup) 65:01/00 1) Copy original to backup 2) Copy backup to original 3) No action [123?q]? pine64-pinephone:~/repos/trail/slim$ # ^ not sure why this flagged pine64-pinephone:~/repos/trail/slim$ # mount the partitions pine64-pinephone:~/repos/trail/slim$ sudo mount /dev/mmcblk2p1 /mnt/emmcboot pine64-pinephone:~/repos/trail/slim$ sudo mount /dev/mmcblk2p2 /mnt/emmcroot pine64-pinephone:~/repos/trail/slim$ # make sure everything looks right pine64-pinephone:~/repos/trail/slim$ ls /mnt/emmcboot boot.scr initramfs sun50i-a64-pinephone-1.1.dtb sun50i-a64-psci.dtbo dtbs lost+found sun50i-a64-pinephone-1.2.dtb vmlinuz pine64-pinephone:~/repos/trail/slim$ ls /mnt/emmcroot bin etc lib lost+found opt sbin usr boot home lib64 media root srv var ``` At this point the setup looked good to me. `pi-agent` running codex-5.1 also poked around and said everything looked right. So I opted to un-mount the SD card and see what happens And... my phone failed to boot :/. It hit this cycle where the LED at the top turned green, then white, then the phone vibrated, then turned green again, then white again, then the phone virated again, etc. Seems like a boot failure to me. The screen did not turn on at all. ## Our goal for right now I want us to focus on debugging the boot and root partitions on EMMC. Setup is described below. You should use tmux to poke around and see what's wrong. Do run diagnostic commands, but don't fix anything - your goal for now is just to understand the problem and come up with theories for why this is happening. We will work together to verify these theories and fix. # Setup I have set up /tmp/mnt/emmcroot and /tmp/mnt/emmcboot on the main GPU box to mirror what we see on the pinephone at /mnt/emmcboot and /mnt/emmcroot. ``` paul-MS-7E16% mkdir -p /tmp/mnt/emmcboot paul-MS-7E16% mkdir -p /tmp/mnt/emmcroot paul-MS-7E16% sshfs user@pinephone:/mnt/emmcboot /tmp/mnt/emmcboot user@pinephone's password: paul-MS-7E16% sshfs user@pinephone:/mnt/emmcroot /tmp/mnt/emmcroot user@pinephone's password: paul-MS-7E16% ls /tmp/mnt/emmcboot boot.scr initramfs sun50i-a64-pinephone-1.1.dtb sun50i-a64-psci.dtbo dtbs lost+found sun50i-a64-pinephone-1.2.dtb vmlinuz paul-MS-7E16% ls /tmp/mnt/emmcroot bin etc lib lost+found opt sbin usr boot home lib64 media root srv var ``` I also have an SSH session to the pinephone at tmux debug:emmc.1 that you can use to poke around the pinephone itself (use the tmux skill). ## Your job Play around. Poke around and see what you can find. Come up with theories for why boot failed. Let me know if you need any help or assisstance.

# Goal I am modding out a pinephone. I will use this pinephone as my main programming device for a long hike. I recently realized that the phone was running 100% off my SD card. The phone has 32Gb of EMMC but is not using any of it Here's the output of 'df'. You can see that there is one device, mmcblk0p2, mounted at root. ```bash pine64-pinephone:~/repos/trail/slim$ df -h / Filesystem Size Used Avail Use% Mounted on /dev/mmcblk0p2 29G 9.7G 18G 36% / ``` And here's the output of lsblk. You can see there are two ~32Gb devices: mmcblk0 (the SD card) and mmcblk2 (the EMMC). The reason I think mmcblk2 is EMMC is because it has a few 'hardcoded' boot partitions (mmcblk2boot0, mmcblk2boot1) that would not exist on the SD card. ```bash pine64-pinephone:~/repos/trail/slim$ lsblk NAME MAJ:MIN RM SIZE RO TYPE MOUNTPOINTS mmcblk0 179:0 0 29.1G 0 disk ├─mmcblk0p1 179:1 0 243M 0 part /boot └─mmcblk0p2 179:2 0 28.9G 0 part / mmcblk2 179:32 0 28.9G 0 disk ├─mmcblk2p1 179:33 0 243M 0 part /mnt/emmcboot └─mmcblk2p2 179:34 0 28.7G 0 part /mnt/emmcroot mmcblk2boot0 179:64 0 4M 1 disk mmcblk2boot1 179:96 0 4M 1 disk zram0 253:0 0 512M 0 disk [SWAP] zram1 253:1 0 2G 0 disk ``` At first, I tried to copy the files from mmcblk0p1 -> mmcblk2p1 and mmcblk0p2 -> mmcblk2p2 directly using `dd`. That looked something like: ```bash sudo dd if=/dev/mmcblk0 or=/dev/mmcblk2 bs=4M status=progress conv=sync,noerror ``` However, when I tried to validate the EMMC partitions I hit an error! Theproblem is that the SD card's root partition is _slightly_ larger than EMMC. When we use 'dd' to copy the file system literally it copies the number of blocks the file system supports. This causes an error since the number of blocks that can be supported on the EMMC is slightly less than what can be supported on the SD card. The option Claude gave me at this point was to downsize the file system on the SD card. This bugged me for a couple reasons though: * It's cumbersome: I don't want to have to downsize the SD card root partition, `dd` copy, then upsize again. * It's annoying. I can't downsize the partition "live" on my pinphone. Instead, I have to take the SD card out of the phone, load it into a machine that has an SD reader, then presumably resize with something like GParted. * ^ because of these it also seems a bit risky. what if I screw up? I do not want to lose progress I told Claude this, and it gave me an alternative solution. Instead of using `dd` to do a block-level copy, we can (a) add file systems to the EMMC partitions directly using `mkfs`, (b) mount those file systems at /mnt/emmcboot, /mnt/emmcroot, and (c) use rsync to copy files over. The whole setup looks roughly like this: ## Set up the boot partition ```bash # use vfat file system for boot partition sudo mkfs.vfat -F32 /dev/mmcblk2p1 # mount that partition to /mnt/emmcboot sudo mkdir -p /mnt/emmcboot sudo mount /dev/mmcblk2p1 /mnt/emmcboot # rsync files from our local system (using SD card) to mounted boot partition sudo rsync -aH --numeric-ids --progress --no-perms --no-owner --no-group /boot/ /mnt/emmcboot/ ``` ## Set up the root partition ```bash # use vfat file system for root partition sudo mkfs.ext4 -F32 /dev/mmcblk2p2 # mount that partition to /mnt/emmcroot sudo mkdir -p /mnt/emmcroot sudo mount /dev/mmcblk2p2 /mnt/emmcroot # rsync files from our local system (using SD card) to mounted boot partition sudo rsync -aHAX --numeric-ids --progress --exclude=/mnt --exclude=/dev --exclude=/tmp --exclude=/proc --exclude=/sys --exclude=/run / /mnt/emmcroot ``` ## Verification ```bash pine64-pinephone:~/repos/trail/slim$ # unmount the partitions pine64-pinephone:~/repos/trail/slim$ sudo umount /mnt/emmcroot pine64-pinephone:~/repos/trail/slim$ sudo umount /mnt/emmcboot pine64-pinephone:~/repos/trail/slim$ # run file system consistency checks pine64-pinephone:~/repos/trail/slim$ sudo fsck.ext4 /dev/mmcblk2p2 e2fsck 1.47.3 (8-Jul-2025) /dev/mmcblk2p2: clean, 184054/1880480 files, 2692856/7515136 blocks pine64-pinephone:~/repos/trail/slim$ su do fsck.vfat /dev/mmcblk2p1 fsck.fat 4.2 (2021-01-31) There are differences between boot sector and its backup. This is mostly harmless. Differences: (offset:original/backup) 65:01/00 1) Copy original to backup 2) Copy backup to original 3) No action [123?q]? pine64-pinephone:~/repos/trail/slim$ # ^ not sure why this flagged pine64-pinephone:~/repos/trail/slim$ # mount the partitions pine64-pinephone:~/repos/trail/slim$ sudo mount /dev/mmcblk2p1 /mnt/emmcboot pine64-pinephone:~/repos/trail/slim$ sudo mount /dev/mmcblk2p2 /mnt/emmcroot pine64-pinephone:~/repos/trail/slim$ # make sure everything looks right pine64-pinephone:~/repos/trail/slim$ ls /mnt/emmcboot boot.scr initramfs sun50i-a64-pinephone-1.1.dtb sun50i-a64-psci.dtbo dtbs lost+found sun50i-a64-pinephone-1.2.dtb vmlinuz pine64-pinephone:~/repos/trail/slim$ ls /mnt/emmcroot bin etc lib lost+found opt sbin usr boot home lib64 media root srv var ``` At this point the setup looked good to me. `pi-agent` running codex-5.1 also poked around and said everything looked right. It hit this cycle where the LED at the top turned green, then white, then the phone vibrated, then turned green again, then white again, then the phone virated again, etc. Seems like a boot failure to me. # Goal I am modding out a pinephone. I will use this pinephone as my main programming device for a long hike. I recently realized that the phone was running 100% off my SD card. The phone has 32Gb of EMMC but is not using any of it Here's the output of 'df'. You can see that there is one device, mmcblk0p2, mounted at root. ```bash pine64-pinephone:~/repos/trail/slim$ df -h / Filesystem Size Used Avail Use% Mounted on /dev/mmcblk0p2 29G 9.7G 18G 36% / ``` And here's the output of lsblk. You can see there are two ~32Gb devices: mmcblk0 (the SD card) and mmcblk2 (the EMMC). The reason I think mmcblk2 is EMMC is because it has a few 'hardcoded' boot partitions (mmcblk2boot0, mmcblk2boot1) that would not exist on the SD card. ```bash pine64-pinephone:~/repos/trail/slim$ lsblk NAME MAJ:MIN RM SIZE RO TYPE MOUNTPOINTS mmcblk0 179:0 0 29.1G 0 disk ├─mmcblk0p1 179:1 0 243M 0 part /boot └─mmcblk0p2 179:2 0 28.9G 0 part / mmcblk2 179:32 0 28.9G 0 disk ├─mmcblk2p1 179:33 0 243M 0 part /mnt/emmcboot └─mmcblk2p2 179:34 0 28.7G 0 part /mnt/emmcroot mmcblk2boot0 179:64 0 4M 1 disk mmcblk2boot1 179:96 0 4M 1 disk zram0 253:0 0 512M 0 disk [SWAP] zram1 253:1 0 2G 0 disk ``` At first, I tried to copy the files from mmcblk0p1 -> mmcblk2p1 and mmcblk0p2 -> mmcblk2p2 directly using `dd`. That looked something like: ```bash sudo dd if=/dev/mmcblk0 or=/dev/mmcblk2 bs=4M status=progress conv=sync,noerror ``` However, when I tried to validate the EMMC partitions I hit an error! Theproblem is that the SD card's root partition is _slightly_ larger than EMMC. When we use 'dd' to copy the file system literally it copies the number of blocks the file system supports. This causes an error since the number of blocks that can be supported on the EMMC is slightly less than what can be supported on the SD card. The option Claude gave me at this point was to downsize the file system on the SD card. This bugged me for a couple reasons though: * It's cumbersome: I don't want to have to downsize the SD card root partition, `dd` copy, then upsize again. * It's annoying. I can't downsize the partition "live" on my pinphone. Instead, I have to take the SD card out of the phone, load it into a machine that has an SD reader, then presumably resize with something like GParted. * ^ because of these it also seems a bit risky. what if I screw up? I do not want to lose progress I told Claude this, and it gave me an alternative solution. Instead of using `dd` to do a block-level copy, we can (a) add file systems to the EMMC partitions directly using `mkfs`, (b) mount those file systems at /mnt/emmcboot, /mnt/emmcroot, and (c) use rsync to copy files over. The whole setup looks roughly like this: ## Set up the boot partition ```bash # use vfat file system for boot partition sudo mkfs.vfat -F32 /dev/mmcblk2p1 # mount that partition to /mnt/emmcboot sudo mkdir -p /mnt/emmcboot sudo mount /dev/mmcblk2p1 /mnt/emmcboot # rsync files from our local system (using SD card) to mounted boot partition sudo rsync -aH --numeric-ids --progress --no-perms --no-owner --no-group /boot/ /mnt/emmcboot/ ``` ## Set up the root partition ```bash # use vfat file system for root partition sudo mkfs.ext4 -F32 /dev/mmcblk2p2 # mount that partition to /mnt/emmcroot sudo mkdir -p /mnt/emmcroot sudo mount /dev/mmcblk2p2 /mnt/emmcroot # rsync files from our local system (using SD card) to mounted boot partition sudo rsync -aHAX --numeric-ids --progress --exclude=/mnt --exclude=/dev --exclude=/tmp --exclude=/proc --exclude=/sys --exclude=/run / /mnt/emmcroot ``` ## Verification ```bash pine64-pinephone:~/repos/trail/slim$ # unmount the partitions pine64-pinephone:~/repos/trail/slim$ sudo umount /mnt/emmcroot pine64-pinephone:~/repos/trail/slim$ sudo umount /mnt/emmcboot pine64-pinephone:~/repos/trail/slim$ # run file system consistency checks pine64-pinephone:~/repos/trail/slim$ sudo fsck.ext4 /dev/mmcblk2p2 e2fsck 1.47.3 (8-Jul-2025) /dev/mmcblk2p2: clean, 184054/1880480 files, 2692856/7515136 blocks pine64-pinephone:~/repos/trail/slim$ su do fsck.vfat /dev/mmcblk2p1 fsck.fat 4.2 (2021-01-31) There are differences between boot sector and its backup. This is mostly harmless. Differences: (offset:original/backup) 65:01/00 1) Copy original to backup 2) Copy backup to original 3) No action [123?q]? pine64-pinephone:~/repos/trail/slim$ # ^ not sure why this flagged pine64-pinephone:~/repos/trail/slim$ # mount the partitions pine64-pinephone:~/repos/trail/slim$ sudo mount /dev/mmcblk2p1 /mnt/emmcboot pine64-pinephone:~/repos/trail/slim$ sudo mount /dev/mmcblk2p2 /mnt/emmcroot pine64-pinephone:~/repos/trail/slim$ # make sure everything looks right pine64-pinephone:~/repos/trail/slim$ ls /mnt/emmcboot boot.scr initramfs sun50i-a64-pinephone-1.1.dtb sun50i-a64-psci.dtbo dtbs lost+found sun50i-a64-pinephone-1.2.dtb vmlinuz pine64-pinephone:~/repos/trail/slim$ ls /mnt/emmcroot bin etc lib lost+found opt sbin usr boot home lib64 media root srv var ``` At this point the setup looked good to me. `pi-agent` running codex-5.1 also poked around and said everything looked right. So I opted to un-mount the SD card and see what happens And... my phone failed to boot :/. It hit this cycle where the LED at the top turned green, then white, then the phone vibrated, then turned green again, then white again, then the phone virated again, etc. Seems like a boot failure to me. The screen did not turn on at all. ## Our goal for right now I want us to focus on debugging the boot and root partitions on EMMC. Setup is described below. --- name: tmux description: How to communicate with tmux panes --- # Background I will often want you to interact with tmux using the tmux cli. * "i am ssh'd into the pinephone at pane 0:1.0. use this pane for interacting with the pinephone" * "send a hello message to the pi agent running on ai:agents.2. This skill explains what I mean when I say things like this. # Interacting with panes Pane interaction is a fundamental part of tmux. There are two primitives `send-keys` and `capture-pane`. `send-keys` gives you the "write" side of the puzzle, `capture-pane` gives you the "read". Together you can do basically everything Example: interacting with a shell ```bash # assume pane 0 in window 'tmux' in session 'skills' is running /bin/bash # i have asked you to tell me what currently shows in the pane # after you've done this, i've asked you to tell me what files exist in the current directory # using 'ls' # capture content of the pane tmux capture-pane -p -t skills:tmux.0 # sometimes you need to see more history tmux capture-pane -p -t skills:tmux.0 -S -500 # run 'ls' in the pane. this is done by literally sending "ls\n" to the pane tmux send-keys -t skills.tmux:0 "ls" Enter # see the result tmux capture-pane -p -t skills:tmux.0 ``` Example: interacting with pi agent ```bash # i have asked you to interrupt the pi agent running at agents:pi.2. i've asked you to do this so you can # introduce yourself. i've asked you to use the name 'bob'. i aksed you to both (a) ask for the agent's name # and land on a communication protocol you can use for inter-agent comms # interrupt the agent because i told you to # wait a second to make sure the esc goes through tmux send-keys -t agents:pi.2 Escape sleep 1 # clear any old prompt by entering backspace 10k times tmux send-keys -t agents:pi.2 -N 10000 BSpace # give the agent your message # need 'Enter' at the end to actually send the message tmux send-keys -t agents:pi.2 "hi. i am 'bob', a pi agent running on paul's system. i'd like you to tell me your name. then, let's figure out ..." Enter # wait a little, then check response # this is tricky, and <5> is subjective. if you don't see any visual change in the agent state # one of at least two things might have happened # # 1. the agent is still processing the response and has not output anything. in this case nothing is wrong - the agent is just taking a long time # 2. the agent is down and will never respond. this is more serious since it mean's the agent you were talking to is effectively dead # # there is no perfect answer for how to handle this. my advice is to poll the agent a few times (say, for up to 30 seconds) to see if state changes. # if not, interrupt it and ask it explicitly for a status update sleep 5 tmux capture-pane -p -t agents:pi.2 ``` What complicates this is that each interactive command you run will have its own CLI interface. For instance, bash will respond to things like signals (ctrl+c, ctrl+z, etc) like you'd expect, but other programs might not. The `bash` and `pi-agent` interfaces are relatively simple. `bash` is as you'd expected (newline delimited, use 'enter' to send commands). `pi-agent` is a bit more complex. There are a few features in `pi-agent` I might ask you to play with that it's worth being aware of: * commands: `pi-agent` supports a bunch of custom commands. you can see a few pop up when you type just '/' (no enter) ```bash tmux send-keys -t agents.pi:2 "/" tmux capture-pane -p -t agents:pi.2 # a few commands should pop up tmux capture-pane -t agents:pi.2 -N 2 Down # navigate to the command you want using arrow keys, which in this case is down 2 from the top ``` you can run a command using '/<command>' with Enter. for instance, to run the settings command: ```bash tmux send-keys -t agents.pi:2 "/settings" Enter tmux capture-pane -p -t agents:pi.2 # setting options should pop up ``` * the menus `pi-agent` uses are interesting. you can actually search the menu by just typing the item you are looking for. for instance, this shows you all commands with 'auto' in them: ```bash # assume you are already in /settings # do NOT use enter here tmux send-keys -t agents.pi:2 "resize" tmux capture-pane -p -t agents:pi.2 # options should pop up ``` * `Escape` will often get you from a sub menu to the home menu * pi agent has a bunch of interesting options that i may ask you to work with (for instance, /tree and /resume). these have more complex menus you should let me know this is your first time using these commands and let me know that, even if you experiment around, you may need help ## Controls Some command line interfaces have custom control documentation that will help you interact with them. `pi-agent`, for instance, has controls at controls/pi-agent/CONTROLS.md that explain how to interact with `pi`. These control interfaces are hard won. Usually I generate these by spinning up an agent in one pane and the CLI I want to generate controls for in the other. I then prompt the agent do use tmux to "do things" in this CLI. I slowly guide the agent so it figures out how the CLI works. At some point, the agent has generated enough internal knowledge that it can write documentation for other agents about how to use the CLI. While this documentation is written by agents for agents, it should follow the general style and conventions of SKILL.md for consistency

bash # assume pane 0 in window 'tmux' in session 'skills' is running /bin/bash # i have asked you to tell me what currently shows in the pane # after you've done this, i've asked you to tell me what files exist in the current directory # using 'ls' # capture content of the pane tmux capture-pane -p -t skills:tmux.0 # sometimes you need to see more history tmux capture-pane -p -t skills:tmux.0 -S -500 # run 'ls' in the pane. this is done by literally sending "ls\n" to the pane tmux send-keys -t skills.tmux:0 "ls" Enter # see the result tmux capture-pane -p -t skills:tmux.0

Thinking:

On the pinephone (tmux pane `debug:emmc.1`) I inspected `/mnt/emmcboot` and confirmed it’s mounted from `/dev/mmcblk2p1` with sane size/usage and contains the expected firmware files. To validate the partition itself I checked the on-disk metadata: - `sudo file -s /dev/mmcblk2p1` verified it’s a valid FAT32 boot sector created by `mkfs.fat`. - `sudo fsck.vfat -n /dev/mmcblk2p1` (non‑destructive) reported only two minor issues: a mismatch between the boot sector and its backup and that the dirty bit was set, both of which are harmless aside from indicating the filesystem wasn’t cleanly unmounted. The run left the filesystem unchanged and reported 73 files/69k clusters free. So structurally the EMMC boot partition looks healthy; the only anomalies are the dirty flag and sector/backup mismatch that `fsck` noted but didn’t repair. With that data we can start reasoning about whether the bootloader is seeing these partitions and whether their contents match the SD card’s expected layout.
iirc we are using vfat for the boot file system. i do not think this aligns with the file system we use for /boot on the SD card. could that cause problems?

Thinking:

Good catch. On the running system `/boot` is currently an `ext2` filesystem (see `cat /etc/fstab` and `blkid` for `/dev/mmcblk0p1`), but the EMMC boot partition `/dev/mmcblk2p1` was freshly `mkfs.vfat`’d. That mismatch is significant: the bootloader (U-Boot on the PinePhone) and the kernel expect to read `boot.scr`, `vmlinuz`, and the DTBs out of an `ext2` layout, the same layout that’s currently on the SD card. If the bootloader is still looking for an `ext2` partition but the device contains a FAT32 filesystem, it won’t find the expected files or metadata (and `boot.scr` won’t be parsed), which could explain the boot failure cycle you saw. So yes, the filesystem type mismatch very likely prevents the EMMC configuration from booting; one theory is simply that nothing on `/dev/mmcblk2p1` is being read because U-Boot is probing for `ext2` but it’s FAT32. Next steps (for later): reformat `/dev/mmcblk2p1` as `ext2`, remount, rsync `/boot`, and confirm U-Boot can actually read it. For now we just need to note the mismatch as a strong hypothesis.
can we use 'dd' to make our lives easier? iirc the boot partition sizes between the SD card and EMMC are the same, it's just the root partition sizes that are different

Thinking:

Yes, for the boot partition it makes sense to go back to `dd` because both partitions are the same size and that’s the thing that broke when we copied the root filesystem—the SD root is slightly larger than the EMMC root, so `dd` overflowed. The boot partition, however, is identical in size on both devices, and copying it block-for-block preserves the ext2 layout U-Boot expects. So the lightweight theory is: if you `dd if=/dev/mmcblk0p1 of=/dev/mmcblk2p1 bs=4M status=progress conv=sync,noerror`, you’ll copy the exact ext2 metadata and data that `/boot` currently uses on the SD card into the EMMC boot partition. That avoids the filesystem type mismatch we created by reformatting to FAT32 and ensures `boot.scr`, `vmlinuz`, DTBs, etc. stay exactly where the bootloader expects them. Before doing that we’d want to confirm: - both partitions really are identical in size (`blockdev --getsz /dev/mmcblk0p1` vs `/dev/mmcblk2p1`). - the EMMC boot partition is unmounted and not in use (so `dd` won’t corrupt a mounted filesystem). - consider backing up the target partition (e.g., `dd if=/dev/mmcblk2p1 of=...`) in case you want to revert. Since you specifically asked to only theorize for now and avoid destructive changes, we can just keep this as the next-step plan: `dd` the SD boot partition to the EMMC boot partition so U-Boot sees an ext2 filesystem with the same content.

ok great. double check to make sure both partitions are equal in size. then, unmount the partition from /mnt/emmcboot (let me know if you need help with this, umount may complain that the mount is in use). i'll give you next steps after that

Thinking:

I was checking that the `dd` copies stuck because the target partition reports “No space left on device” even though the source and target sizes match (254803968 bytes). To rule out differences in block alignment or stray buffered data, I tried to compute `254803968 % 4096` to see if there was any remainder requiring extra padding. is 'bs' too big? maybe 4M is too large? also how do we get the no space left on device error in the first place? do we have to 'clear' the device before writing to it?

Thinking:

The “No space left on device” came from the last write: with `conv=sync` and `bs=4M`, every read block is forced to be exactly 4 MB when written. The source partition isn’t an exact multiple of 4 MB, so on the final read you get a partial block that `dd` pads to 4 MB. That padded block still gets written, so you end up trying to write slightly more than the 254,803,968 bytes the target can hold, hence the ENOSPC. `bs=4M` isn’t inherently too big—it just means there’s a partial chunk at the end. The fix is to drop `conv=sync` (or avoid padding the last block). For example: ```bash sudo dd if=/dev/mmcblk0p1 of=/dev/mmcblk2p1 bs=4M status=progress conv=noerror ``` This way the final partial block is written as-is, and `dd` stops exactly at the partition’s size so you won’t overrun it. There’s no need to “clear” the device first; `dd` just overwrites the raw partition.

ok. check the output again: dropping 'sync' did fix the issue. what does 'conv=sync' do again? i do not understand that bit

Thinking:

Dropping `conv=sync` fixed the “No space left” because `conv=sync` tells `dd` to pad every input block to the full `bs` size before writing. That’s useful when you’re copying from a source that has holes or partial blocks (e.g., copying a raw disk image from a sparse file), because it guarantees the output stays block-aligned. But when the source partition isn’t an exact multiple of 4 MB, `conv=sync` pads the last (partial) block up to 4 MB, so you end up writing more bytes than the destination partition can hold—hence ENOSPC. Without `conv=sync`, `dd` writes whatever it actually read, and the final partial block doesn’t get padded. That means as soon as it finishes the source partition, it stops, and you avoid overrunning the slightly-smaller target. So we keep `bs=4M` for speed but leave out `conv=sync` when copying partition-to-partition data that must fit exactly.
will this cause any issues?

Thinking:

No, dropping `conv=sync` here won’t cause issues. It just means `dd` writes exactly what it reads, so the last partial block doesn’t get padded to 4 MB. That’s exactly what we want when copying partition-to-partition data where the destination is the same size (or even slightly smaller)—it prevents us from overrunning the device. The only time you really need `conv=sync` is when you’re trying to maintain fixed-size blocks (e.g., when restoring a disk image into a sparse file or when you know the destination expects every block to be full). For a straight copy between matching partitions, leaving it out is safe and avoids the ENOSPC error you saw.

Thinking:

Exactly—now that the boot partition has an ext2 layout again, you can mount it and run `fsck.ext2` to verify the filesystem is healthy before booting from EMMC. Example steps: ```bash # mount it somewhere (e.g., /mnt/emmcboot) sudo mount /dev/mmcblk2p1 /mnt/emmcboot # run a safety check (unmount first if `fsck.ext2` complains about being mounted) sudo umount /mnt/emmcboot sudo fsck.ext2 -f /dev/mmcblk2p1 ``` You can skip the `-f` if you just want a non-destructive check, but forcing it ensures a thorough scan. After `fsck` finishes, remount it if you need to peek at the contents again.

Thinking:

Good catch: yes, because we `dd`’d the SD boot partition onto the EMMC one, the ext2 superblock (including its UUID) was copied verbatim. `file -s /dev/mmcblk2p1` now reports `UUID=49e13d5b-f10c-4bbc-94aa-ce673b21e141`, exactly the same as `/dev/mmcblk0p1`. That’s expected—`dd` clones every byte, so the filesystem UUID doesn’t automatically change. This can cause confusion if both partitions are present simultaneously, because tools (and the kernel) may see two filesystems with the same UUID and not know which is which. To avoid that, once you’ve verified the copy you can give the EMMC boot partition a new UUID (e.g., `sudo tune2fs -U random /dev/mmcblk2p1`) and then update `/etc/fstab` to mount that new UUID at `/boot` when the system switches to the EMMC root. Alternatively, you can keep the current UUID but only ever mount the EMMC boot partition once you’ve removed the SD card; the race only happens while both disks are attached. Let me know which path you’d prefer (new UUID vs. relying on `/mnt/emmcboot` path), and I can help update `fstab` accordingly.
weird. 'blkid' is saying that /dev/mmcblk2p1 has type 'vfat' with UUID 19A9-244F, while 'file -s' is saying ext2 with the same UUID as /dev/mmcblk0p1

Thinking:

`file -s` is reading the actual bytes you just `dd`’d and sees the ext2 superblock (with UUID `49e13d5b…`), so on-disk data is definitely ext2. `blkid`, on the other hand, caches previous probe results (and also looks at the partition-type byte), so it still reports the vfat signature it saw before the `dd`. That’s why you’re seeing the discrepancy. To refresh the kernel/udev view you can re-probe the device: ```bash sudo blkid -g /dev/mmcblk2p1 # clears blkid cache for the device sudo udevadm trigger -v --action=change /dev/mmcblk2p1 sudo blkid /dev/mmcblk2p1 # should now show TYPE="ext2" and the ext2 UUID ``` Once `blkid` agrees with `file -s`, you’ll know the fs metadata is consistent. If you’d rather keep the boot UUID unique, we can use `tune2fs -U random /dev/mmcblk2p1` after that and then update `/etc/fstab` accordingly. Let me know which direction you want to take.

Thinking:

Ran the refresh sequence you asked for: `sudo blkid -g /dev/mmcblk2p1`, `sudo udevadm trigger ...`, and then checked with `blkid`. Because `blkid` persisted the previous FAT metadata, I also ran `sudo blkid -c /dev/null /dev/mmcblk2p1`, which forced it to probe the superblock. That call now reports the ext2 UUID (`49e13d5b-f10c-4bbc-94aa-ce673b21e141`) that matches the SD card, confirming the partition’s type and UUID from the `dd` copy.
but 'blkid /dev/mmcblk2p1' still says type of vfat?

Thinking:

Because `blkid` normally reads from its cache (`/etc/blkid.tab`) and the partition table entry still advertises a FAT type, the plain `blkid /dev/mmcblk2p1` output keeps showing `TYPE="vfat"`. When you force a fresh probe with `sudo blkid -c /dev/null /dev/mmcblk2p1` (which I ran earlier), it re-scans the on-disk superblock and now shows the ext2 metadata (`UUID=49e13d5b-…`, `TYPE="ext2"`). So the underlying filesystem really is ext2; it’s just the cached/cached scan that still reflects the old FAT data. You can keep using the `-c /dev/null` probe or delete `/etc/blkid.tab` if you want `blkid` to forget the old entry.
ok weird. so 'blkid' by itself will return results from cache, but 'blkid -c /dev/null ...' will lookup results dynamically? is that right?

Thinking:

Exactly: `blkid` normally consults its cache (`/etc/blkid.tab`), so running it without options can return stale info about `/dev/mmcblk2p1`. When you pass `-c /dev/null`, you tell it not to use a cache file, forcing it to rescan the device’s superblock and report the current metadata (which now shows the ext2 filesystem you just copied). So yes, `blkid -c /dev/null /dev/mmcblk2p1` is the command to use when you want a guaranteed live probe instead of cached data.

Thinking:

If the phone still fails to boot from EMMC after these changes, we can fall back to a systematic debugging loop: 1. **Collect early boot output** – Attach serial or HDMI if available, or capture the green/white LED/bootloader VGA messages via UART to see where it stops (U-Boot vs. kernel vs. initramfs). 2. **Verify bootloader config** – Check `boot.scr` (and its decompiled `boot.txt`) to ensure it points to the right rootfs UUID and device (`mmcblk2p2`). Compare with the SD card’s version to spot differences. 3. **Inspect kernel cmdline/initramfs** – Make sure the kernel command line inside the `extlinux`/`boot.scr` matches the EMMC devices, and that any init scripts expect `/dev/mmcblk2p2`. 4. **Mount EMMC root from another system** – Use the mirrored `/tmp/mnt/emmcroot` setup you described to check `/etc/fstab`, `/etc/factory`, `grub`/`fstab` references, and to ensure `init` will mount the right root filesystem. 5. **Look at log files** – If we can boot from SD again, examine `dmesg`, `journalctl` (if available), `/var/log/boot.log`, or `pmbootstrap` logs to see failure clues from the last EMMC attempt. 6. **Use a recovery shell** – If the phone drops to initramfs or a recovery prompt, run `lsblk`, `cat /proc/cmdline`, and `mount` there to confirm what partitions it sees and tries to use. 7. **Confirm filesystem integrity** – Re-run `fsck` on both partitions, maybe in a live environment, to rule out corruption that might only show up during boot. 8. **Fallback plan** – Keep the SD-based system intact so you can still boot the phone and iterate. Once we identify a specific subsystem (bootloader config, kernel command line, root overlay), we can make targeted edits instead of broadly copying files. Let me know if you want me to start preparing logs or scripts for any of those steps.

# <file system> <mount point> <type> <options> <dump> <pass> # you can get this using 'sudo blkid /dev/mmcblk2p1' UUID=c880538a-186e-4374-925f-9e25ca66546a / ext4 defaults 0 0 # nodev - "do not interpret device special files on this file system". apparently this is to prevent the case where someone creates a fake device on /boot in that the kernel will not treat it as a real device # nosuid - "ignore set-user-id and set-group-id bits on this file system". this disables setuid and setgid permissions that could normally cause binaries to run with elevated privledges # noexec - "do not allow direct execution of binaries on this filesystem" UUID=19A9-244F /boot vfat nodev,nosuid,noexec 0 0

It sets `bootdev` to `2` whenever it boots from eMMC, and the `bootargs` it builds point to `/dev/mmcblk${bootdev}p1` for `/boot` and `/dev/mmcblk${bootdev}p2` for the rootfs. So if the bootloader actually lands on eMMC, it will look at the correct devices (mmcblk2p1/mmcblk2p2). 2. On the mirrored root (`/tmp/mnt/emmcroot/etc/fstab`) I confirmed the `UUID=c880538a-186e-4374-925f-9e25ca66546a` entry is already configured for `/`, so once the system mounts `mmcblk2p2` it will use the EMMC root. The `/boot` line in that mirror still references the old `19A9-244F` vfat UUID; once the boot partition walks over to ext2 we’ll want to update that file to match the new `41c1be64-5808-41b1-8eb9-2519d9cea174` UUID so it stays consistent. # Goal I am modding out a pinephone. I will use this pinephone as my main programming device for a long hike. I recently realized that the phone was running 100% off my SD card. The phone has 32Gb of EMMC but is not using any of it Here's the output of 'df'. You can see that there is one device, mmcblk0p2, mounted at root. ```bash pine64-pinephone:~/repos/trail/slim$ df -h / Filesystem Size Used Avail Use% Mounted on /dev/mmcblk0p2 29G 9.7G 18G 36% / ``` And here's the output of lsblk. You can see there are two ~32Gb devices: mmcblk0 (the SD card) and mmcblk2 (the EMMC). The reason I think mmcblk2 is EMMC is because it has a few 'hardcoded' boot partitions (mmcblk2boot0, mmcblk2boot1) that would not exist on the SD card. ```bash pine64-pinephone:~/repos/trail/slim$ lsblk NAME MAJ:MIN RM SIZE RO TYPE MOUNTPOINTS mmcblk0 179:0 0 29.1G 0 disk ├─mmcblk0p1 179:1 0 243M 0 part /boot └─mmcblk0p2 179:2 0 28.9G 0 part / mmcblk2 179:32 0 28.9G 0 disk ├─mmcblk2p1 179:33 0 243M 0 part /mnt/emmcboot └─mmcblk2p2 179:34 0 28.7G 0 part /mnt/emmcroot mmcblk2boot0 179:64 0 4M 1 disk mmcblk2boot1 179:96 0 4M 1 disk zram0 253:0 0 512M 0 disk [SWAP] zram1 253:1 0 2G 0 disk ``` At first, I tried to copy the files from mmcblk0p1 -> mmcblk2p1 and mmcblk0p2 -> mmcblk2p2 directly using `dd`. That looked something like: ```bash sudo dd if=/dev/mmcblk0 or=/dev/mmcblk2 bs=4M status=progress conv=sync,noerror ``` However, when I tried to validate the EMMC partitions I hit an error! Theproblem is that the SD card's root partition is _slightly_ larger than EMMC. When we use 'dd' to copy the file system literally it copies the number of blocks the file system supports. This causes an error since the number of blocks that can be supported on the EMMC is slightly less than what can be supported on the SD card. The option Claude gave me at this point was to downsize the file system on the SD card. This bugged me for a couple reasons though: * It's cumbersome: I don't want to have to downsize the SD card root partition, `dd` copy, then upsize again. * It's annoying. I can't downsize the partition "live" on my pinphone. Instead, I have to take the SD card out of the phone, load it into a machine that has an SD reader, then presumably resize with something like GParted. * ^ because of these it also seems a bit risky. what if I screw up? I do not want to lose progress I told Claude this, and it gave me an alternative solution. Instead of using `dd` to do a block-level copy, we can (a) add file systems to the EMMC partitions directly using `mkfs`, (b) mount those file systems at /mnt/emmcboot, /mnt/emmcroot, and (c) use rsync to copy files over. The whole setup looks roughly like this: ## Set up the boot partition ```bash # use vfat file system for boot partition sudo mkfs.vfat -F32 /dev/mmcblk2p1 # mount that partition to /mnt/emmcboot sudo mkdir -p /mnt/emmcboot sudo mount /dev/mmcblk2p1 /mnt/emmcboot # rsync files from our local system (using SD card) to mounted boot partition sudo rsync -aH --numeric-ids --progress --no-perms --no-owner --no-group /boot/ /mnt/emmcboot/ ``` ## Set up the root partition ```bash # use vfat file system for root partition sudo mkfs.ext4 -F32 /dev/mmcblk2p2 # mount that partition to /mnt/emmcroot sudo mkdir -p /mnt/emmcroot sudo mount /dev/mmcblk2p2 /mnt/emmcroot # rsync files from our local system (using SD card) to mounted boot partition sudo rsync -aHAX --numeric-ids --progress --exclude=/mnt --exclude=/dev --exclude=/tmp --exclude=/proc --exclude=/sys --exclude=/run / /mnt/emmcroot ``` ## Verification ```bash pine64-pinephone:~/repos/trail/slim$ # unmount the partitions pine64-pinephone:~/repos/trail/slim$ sudo umount /mnt/emmcroot pine64-pinephone:~/repos/trail/slim$ sudo umount /mnt/emmcboot pine64-pinephone:~/repos/trail/slim$ # run file system consistency checks pine64-pinephone:~/repos/trail/slim$ sudo fsck.ext4 /dev/mmcblk2p2 e2fsck 1.47.3 (8-Jul-2025) /dev/mmcblk2p2: clean, 184054/1880480 files, 2692856/7515136 blocks pine64-pinephone:~/repos/trail/slim$ su do fsck.vfat /dev/mmcblk2p1 fsck.fat 4.2 (2021-01-31) There are differences between boot sector and its backup. This is mostly harmless. Differences: (offset:original/backup) 65:01/00 1) Copy original to backup 2) Copy backup to original 3) No action [123?q]? pine64-pinephone:~/repos/trail/slim$ # ^ not sure why this flagged pine64-pinephone:~/repos/trail/slim$ # mount the partitions pine64-pinephone:~/repos/trail/slim$ sudo mount /dev/mmcblk2p1 /mnt/emmcboot pine64-pinephone:~/repos/trail/slim$ sudo mount /dev/mmcblk2p2 /mnt/emmcroot pine64-pinephone:~/repos/trail/slim$ # make sure everything looks right pine64-pinephone:~/repos/trail/slim$ ls /mnt/emmcboot boot.scr initramfs sun50i-a64-pinephone-1.1.dtb sun50i-a64-psci.dtbo dtbs lost+found sun50i-a64-pinephone-1.2.dtb vmlinuz pine64-pinephone:~/repos/trail/slim$ ls /mnt/emmcroot bin etc lib lost+found opt sbin usr boot home lib64 media root srv var ``` At this point the setup looked good to me. `pi-agent` running codex-5.1 also poked around and said everything looked right. So I opted to un-mount the SD card and see what happens And... my phone failed to boot :/. It hit this cycle where the LED at the top turned green, then white, then the phone vibrated, then turned green again, then white again, then the phone virated again, etc. Seems like a boot failure to me. The screen did not turn on at all. ## Our goal for right now I want us to focus on debugging the boot and root partitions on EMMC. Setup is described below. You should use tmux to poke around and see what's wrong. Do run diagnostic commands, but don't fix anything - your goal for now is just to understand the problem and come up with theories for why this is happening. We will work together to verify these theories and fix. # Setup I have set up /tmp/mnt/emmcroot and /tmp/mnt/emmcboot on the main GPU box to mirror what we see on the pinephone at /mnt/emmcboot and /mnt/emmcroot. ``` paul-MS-7E16% mkdir -p /tmp/mnt/emmcboot paul-MS-7E16% mkdir -p /tmp/mnt/emmcroot paul-MS-7E16% sshfs user@pinephone:/mnt/emmcboot /tmp/mnt/emmcboot user@pinephone's password: paul-MS-7E16% sshfs user@pinephone:/mnt/emmcroot /tmp/mnt/emmcroot user@pinephone's password: paul-MS-7E16% ls /tmp/mnt/emmcboot boot.scr initramfs sun50i-a64-pinephone-1.1.dtb sun50i-a64-psci.dtbo dtbs lost+found sun50i-a64-pinephone-1.2.dtb vmlinuz paul-MS-7E16% ls /tmp/mnt/emmcroot bin etc lib lost+found opt sbin usr boot home lib64 media root srv var ``` I also have an SSH session to the pinephone at tmux debug:emmc.1 that you can use to poke around the pinephone itself (use the tmux skill). ## Your job Play around. Poke around and see what you can find. Come up with theories for why boot failed. Let me know if you need any help or assisstance.

# Goal I am modding out a pinephone. I will use this pinephone as my main programming device for a long hike. I recently realized that the phone was running 100% off my SD card. The phone has 32Gb of EMMC but is not using any of it Here's the output of 'df'. You can see that there is one device, mmcblk0p2, mounted at root. ```bash pine64-pinephone:~/repos/trail/slim$ df -h / Filesystem Size Used Avail Use% Mounted on /dev/mmcblk0p2 29G 9.7G 18G 36% / ``` And here's the output of lsblk. You can see there are two ~32Gb devices: mmcblk0 (the SD card) and mmcblk2 (the EMMC). The reason I think mmcblk2 is EMMC is because it has a few 'hardcoded' boot partitions (mmcblk2boot0, mmcblk2boot1) that would not exist on the SD card. ```bash pine64-pinephone:~/repos/trail/slim$ lsblk NAME MAJ:MIN RM SIZE RO TYPE MOUNTPOINTS mmcblk0 179:0 0 29.1G 0 disk ├─mmcblk0p1 179:1 0 243M 0 part /boot └─mmcblk0p2 179:2 0 28.9G 0 part / mmcblk2 179:32 0 28.9G 0 disk ├─mmcblk2p1 179:33 0 243M 0 part /mnt/emmcboot └─mmcblk2p2 179:34 0 28.7G 0 part /mnt/emmcroot mmcblk2boot0 179:64 0 4M 1 disk mmcblk2boot1 179:96 0 4M 1 disk zram0 253:0 0 512M 0 disk [SWAP] zram1 253:1 0 2G 0 disk ``` At first, I tried to copy the files from mmcblk0p1 -> mmcblk2p1 and mmcblk0p2 -> mmcblk2p2 directly using `dd`. That looked something like: ```bash sudo dd if=/dev/mmcblk0 or=/dev/mmcblk2 bs=4M status=progress conv=sync,noerror ``` However, when I tried to validate the EMMC partitions I hit an error! Theproblem is that the SD card's root partition is _slightly_ larger than EMMC. When we use 'dd' to copy the file system literally it copies the number of blocks the file system supports. This causes an error since the number of blocks that can be supported on the EMMC is slightly less than what can be supported on the SD card. The option Claude gave me at this point was to downsize the file system on the SD card. This bugged me for a couple reasons though: * It's cumbersome: I don't want to have to downsize the SD card root partition, `dd` copy, then upsize again. * It's annoying. I can't downsize the partition "live" on my pinphone. Instead, I have to take the SD card out of the phone, load it into a machine that has an SD reader, then presumably resize with something like GParted. * ^ because of these it also seems a bit risky. what if I screw up? I do not want to lose progress I told Claude this, and it gave me an alternative solution. Instead of using `dd` to do a block-level copy, we can (a) add file systems to the EMMC partitions directly using `mkfs`, (b) mount those file systems at /mnt/emmcboot, /mnt/emmcroot, and (c) use rsync to copy files over. The whole setup looks roughly like this: ## Set up the boot partition ```bash # use vfat file system for boot partition sudo mkfs.vfat -F32 /dev/mmcblk2p1 # mount that partition to /mnt/emmcboot sudo mkdir -p /mnt/emmcboot sudo mount /dev/mmcblk2p1 /mnt/emmcboot # rsync files from our local system (using SD card) to mounted boot partition sudo rsync -aH --numeric-ids --progress --no-perms --no-owner --no-group /boot/ /mnt/emmcboot/ ``` ## Set up the root partition ```bash # use vfat file system for root partition sudo mkfs.ext4 -F32 /dev/mmcblk2p2 # mount that partition to /mnt/emmcroot sudo mkdir -p /mnt/emmcroot sudo mount /dev/mmcblk2p2 /mnt/emmcroot # rsync files from our local system (using SD card) to mounted boot partition sudo rsync -aHAX --numeric-ids --progress --exclude=/mnt --exclude=/dev --exclude=/tmp --exclude=/proc --exclude=/sys --exclude=/run / /mnt/emmcroot ``` ## Verification ```bash pine64-pinephone:~/repos/trail/slim$ # unmount the partitions pine64-pinephone:~/repos/trail/slim$ sudo umount /mnt/emmcroot pine64-pinephone:~/repos/trail/slim$ sudo umount /mnt/emmcboot pine64-pinephone:~/repos/trail/slim$ # run file system consistency checks pine64-pinephone:~/repos/trail/slim$ sudo fsck.ext4 /dev/mmcblk2p2 e2fsck 1.47.3 (8-Jul-2025) /dev/mmcblk2p2: clean, 184054/1880480 files, 2692856/7515136 blocks pine64-pinephone:~/repos/trail/slim$ su do fsck.vfat /dev/mmcblk2p1 fsck.fat 4.2 (2021-01-31) There are differences between boot sector and its backup. This is mostly harmless. Differences: (offset:original/backup) 65:01/00 1) Copy original to backup 2) Copy backup to original 3) No action [123?q]? pine64-pinephone:~/repos/trail/slim$ # ^ not sure why this flagged pine64-pinephone:~/repos/trail/slim$ # mount the partitions pine64-pinephone:~/repos/trail/slim$ sudo mount /dev/mmcblk2p1 /mnt/emmcboot pine64-pinephone:~/repos/trail/slim$ sudo mount /dev/mmcblk2p2 /mnt/emmcroot pine64-pinephone:~/repos/trail/slim$ # make sure everything looks right pine64-pinephone:~/repos/trail/slim$ ls /mnt/emmcboot boot.scr initramfs sun50i-a64-pinephone-1.1.dtb sun50i-a64-psci.dtbo dtbs lost+found sun50i-a64-pinephone-1.2.dtb vmlinuz pine64-pinephone:~/repos/trail/slim$ ls /mnt/emmcroot bin etc lib lost+found opt sbin usr boot home lib64 media root srv var ``` At this point the setup looked good to me. `pi-agent` running codex-5.1 also poked around and said everything looked right. So I opted to un-mount the SD card and see what happens And... my phone failed to boot :/. It hit this cycle where the LED at the top turned green, then white, then the phone vibrated, then turned green again, then white again, then the phone virated again, etc. Seems like a boot failure to me. The screen did not turn on at all. ## Our goal for right now I want us to focus on debugging the boot and root partitions on EMMC. Setup is described below. You should use tmux to poke around and see what's wrong. Do run diagnostic commands, but don't fix anything - your goal for now is just to understand the problem and come up with theories for why this is happening. We will work together to verify these theories and fix. # Setup I have set up /tmp/mnt/emmcroot and /tmp/mnt/emmcboot on the main GPU box to mirror what we see on the pinephone at /mnt/emmcboot and /mnt/emmcroot. ``` paul-MS-7E16% mkdir -p /tmp/mnt/emmcboot paul-MS-7E16% mkdir -p /tmp/mnt/emmcroot paul-MS-7E16% sshfs user@pinephone:/mnt/emmcboot /tmp/mnt/emmcboot user@pinephone's password: paul-MS-7E16% sshfs user@pinephone:/mnt/emmcroot /tmp/mnt/emmcroot user@pinephone's password: paul-MS-7E16% ls /tmp/mnt/emmcboot boot.scr initramfs sun50i-a64-pinephone-1.1.dtb sun50i-a64-psci.dtbo dtbs lost+found sun50i-a64-pinephone-1.2.dtb vmlinuz paul-MS-7E16% ls /tmp/mnt/emmcroot bin etc lib lost+found opt sbin usr boot home lib64 media root srv var ``` I also have an SSH session to the pinephone at tmux debug:emmc.1 that you can use to poke around the pinephone itself (use the tmux skill). ## Your job Play around. Poke around and see what you can find. Come up with theories for why boot failed. Let me know if you need any help or assisstance.

Theproblem is that the SD card's root partition is _slightly_ larger than EMMC. When we use 'dd' to copy the file system literally it copies the number of blocks the file system supports. This causes an error since the number of blocks that can be supported on the EMMC is slightly less than what can be supported on the SD card. The option Claude gave me at this point was to downsize the file system on the SD card. This bugged me for a couple reasons though: * It's cumbersome: I don't want to have to downsize the SD card root partition, `dd` copy, then upsize again. * It's annoying. I can't downsize the partition "live" on my pinphone. Instead, I have to take the SD card out of the phone, load it into a machine that has an SD reader, then presumably resize with something like GParted. * ^ because of these it also seems a bit risky. what if I screw up? I do not want to lose progress I told Claude this, and it gave me an alternative solution. Instead of using `dd` to do a block-level copy, we can (a) add file systems to the EMMC partitions directly using `mkfs`, (b) mount those file systems at /mnt/emmcboot, /mnt/emmcroot, and (c) use rsync to copy files over. The whole setup looks roughly like this: ## Set up the boot partition ```bash # use vfat file system for boot partition sudo mkfs.vfat -F32 /dev/mmcblk2p1 # mount that partition to /mnt/emmcboot sudo mkdir -p /mnt/emmcboot sudo mount /dev/mmcblk2p1 /mnt/emmcboot # rsync files from our local system (using SD card) to mounted boot partition sudo rsync -aH --numeric-ids --progress --no-perms --no-owner --no-group /boot/ /mnt/emmcboot/ ``` ## Set up the root partition ```bash # use vfat file system for root partition sudo mkfs.ext4 -F32 /dev/mmcblk2p2 # mount that partition to /mnt/emmcroot sudo mkdir -p /mnt/emmcroot sudo mount /dev/mmcblk2p2 /mnt/emmcroot # rsync files from our local system (using SD card) to mounted boot partition sudo rsync -aHAX --numeric-ids --progress --exclude=/mnt --exclude=/dev --exclude=/tmp --exclude=/proc --exclude=/sys --exclude=/run / /mnt/emmcroot ``` ## Verification ```bash pine64-pinephone:~/repos/trail/slim$ # unmount the partitions pine64-pinephone:~/repos/trail/slim$ sudo umount /mnt/emmcroot pine64-pinephone:~/repos/trail/slim$ sudo umount /mnt/emmcboot pine64-pinephone:~/repos/trail/slim$ # run file system consistency checks pine64-pinephone:~/repos/trail/slim$ sudo fsck.ext4 /dev/mmcblk2p2 e2fsck 1.47.3 (8-Jul-2025) /dev/mmcblk2p2: clean, 184054/1880480 files, 2692856/7515136 blocks pine64-pinephone:~/repos/trail/slim$ su do fsck.vfat /dev/mmcblk2p1 fsck.fat 4.2 (2021-01-31) There are differences between boot sector and its backup. This is mostly harmless. Differences: (offset:original/backup) 65:01/00 1) Copy original to backup 2) Copy backup to original 3) No action [123?q]? pine64-pinephone:~/repos/trail/slim$ # ^ not sure why this flagged pine64-pinephone:~/repos/trail/slim$ # mount the partitions pine64-pinephone:~/repos/trail/slim$ sudo mount /dev/mmcblk2p1 /mnt/emmcboot pine64-pinephone:~/repos/trail/slim$ sudo mount /dev/mmcblk2p2 /mnt/emmcroot pine64-pinephone:~/repos/trail/slim$ # make sure everything looks right pine64-pinephone:~/repos/trail/slim$ ls /mnt/emmcboot boot.scr initramfs sun50i-a64-pinephone-1.1.dtb sun50i-a64-psci.dtbo dtbs lost+found sun50i-a64-pinephone-1.2.dtb vmlinuz pine64-pinephone:~/repos/trail/slim$ ls /mnt/emmcroot bin etc lib lost+found opt sbin usr boot home lib64 media root srv var ``` At this point the setup looked good to me. `pi-agent` running codex-5.1 also poked around and said everything looked right. So I opted to un-mount the SD card and see what happens And... my phone failed to boot :/. It hit this cycle where the LED at the top turned green, then white, then the phone vibrated, then turned green again, then white again, then the phone virated again, etc. Seems like a boot failure to me. The screen did not turn on at all. ## Our goal for right now I want us to focus on debugging the boot and root partitions on EMMC. Setup is described below. You should use tmux to poke around and see what's wrong. Do run diagnostic commands, but don't fix anything - your goal for now is just to understand the problem and come up with theories for why this is happening. We will work together to verify these theories and fix. # Setup I have set up /tmp/mnt/emmcroot and /tmp/mnt/emmcboot on the main GPU box to mirror what we see on the pinephone at /mnt/emmcboot and /mnt/emmcroot. ``` paul-MS-7E16% mkdir -p /tmp/mnt/emmcboot paul-MS-7E16% mkdir -p /tmp/mnt/emmcroot paul-MS-7E16% sshfs user@pinephone:/mnt/emmcboot /tmp/mnt/emmcboot user@pinephone's password: paul-MS-7E16% sshfs user@pinephone:/mnt/emmcroot /tmp/mnt/emmcroot user@pinephone's password: paul-MS-7E16% ls /tmp/mnt/emmcboot boot.scr initramfs sun50i-a64-pinephone-1.1.dtb sun50i-a64-psci.dtbo dtbs lost+found sun50i-a64-pinephone-1.2.dtb vmlinuz paul-MS-7E16% ls /tmp/mnt/emmcroot bin etc lib lost+found opt sbin usr boot home lib64 media root srv var ``` I also have an SSH session to the pinephone at tmux debug:emmc.1 that you can use to poke around the pinephone itself (use the tmux skill). ## Your job Play around. Poke around and see what you can find. Come up with theories for why boot failed. Let me know if you need any help or assisstance.

We fixed that: in the PinePhone shell (`tmux debug:emmc.1`) I edited `/mnt/emmcroot/etc/fstab` to replace the old `UUID=19A9-244F /boot vfat…` line with the new ext2 UUID (`41c1be64-5808-41b1-8eb9-2519d9cea174 /boot ext2…`). After the change the file now references the right identifiers for both `/` and `/boot`, and `blkid` confirms both partitions match those UUIDs. With that corrected, the logs under `/mnt/emmcroot/var/log` should now reflect just the EMMC devices. Thinking:

The reason you still see lines about `EXT4-fs (mmcblk0p1)`/UUID `49e13…` inside `/mnt/emmcroot/var/log/dmesg` is because that log file has history from the SD-card boot path. When you inspected it earlier, the phone had been booted from the SD card, so the kernel was mounting `/boot` from `mmcblk0p1` and writing those entries. The log lives on the root filesystem, so now that we’re looking at `/mnt/emmcroot` (the EMMC root) it still contains all those past messages unless you truncate or rotate the log. After you boot purely from eMMC with the updated `/etc/fstab`, future `dmesg` entries written to that path will reference `mmcblk2p1`/`mmcblk2p2` instead. The one other explanation you were touching on is that while the phone was trying to boot from EMMC previously, its `/etc/fstab` still pointed `/boot` at the SD-backed UUID, so the initramfs tried to mount `mmcblk0p1` even though the SD card wasn’t present. That’s why the log would show it again during a pure-EMMC attempt—not because the log was lying, but because the system literally executed a mount of that UUID. Now that we fixed `/mnt/emmcroot/etc/fstab`, the next boot should only touch the EMMC UUIDs, and the log you inspect on that filesystem will reflect the EMMC devices only.
oh good point! speaking of should i wipe any log files before re-running boot from emmc?

Thinking:

No need to wipe them—unless you specifically want a clean slate for easier triage, you can just move or truncate the existing `var/log/dmesg`/`journal` files before rebooting. That way the next logs only contain messages from the new boot. For example: ```bash sudo truncate -s 0 /mnt/emmcroot/var/log/dmesg sudo rm -rf /mnt/emmcroot/var/log/journal/* ``` (or move them aside with `mv … /mnt/emmcroot/var/log/dmesg.old`). That’s optional, though—existing logs don’t hurt anything, they just make reading new failures a little noisier. Let me know if you’d like me to clear them for you before the next boot attempt.

Thinking:

If waiting that long still leaves you stuck on the splash, switching to a minimal boot target (like single-user/shell) can be really helpful to see where things stop. On the PinePhone you can do that by editing the U-Boot command line on the SD-free boot: 1. Boot up normally, then interrupt U-Boot (hold Volume Up) so you get to the prompt. 2. Use `setenv bootargs` to append `init=/bin/sh` (or `systemd.unit=rescue.target` if you prefer systemd-style) so the kernel drops you straight into a shell. 3. Boot with that and you’ll land in a minimal shell on the EMMC root; from there you can inspect `/var/log` in real time, run `dmesg`, or start services manually to see what’s failing. If you’d rather stay with logs, we can tail `/mnt/emmcroot/var/log` right after the next hang or look at `journalctl` inside the EMMC root (you can install it or use `chroot /mnt/emmcroot /bin/sh` to run commands there). Let me know which direction you want to take next and I’ll guide you through it step by step.

Thinking:

Here’s what’s happening now: the kernel command line (`cat /proc/cmdline`) still says `pmos_boot=/dev/mmcblk0p1` and `pmos_root=/dev/mmcblk0p2`, so the kernel is mounting the SD card root even though you’ve already cloned EMMC and removed the SD card. That means the bootloader’s U-Boot script is still running with `bootdev=0`. The script you saw in `/mnt/emmcboot/boot.scr` sets `pmos_root`/`pmos_boot` from `mmcblk${bootdev}`, and `bootdev` is being left at 0 because the U-Boot environment (or the compiled script logic) decides “booting from SD” even though there’s no card. Because `/etc/fw_env.config` doesn’t exist on this install, we can’t easily use `fw_printenv`/`fw_setenv` to flip the `mmc_bootdev` variable. The simplest fix is to edit the boot script on the EMMC boot partition so it forces `bootdev=2` (or explicitly hard-codes `pmos_root=/dev/mmcblk2p2` and `pmos_boot=/dev/mmcblk2p1`) before it builds `bootargs`. You can do that by: 1. Writing a new `boot.txt` that sets the right `bootdev` or root variables (you can base it on the current script—the `strings` output from `boot.scr` shows what it’s doing). 2. Rebuilding the script with `mkimage` and replacing `/mnt/emmcboot/boot.scr` (e.g., `mkimage -A arm -O linux -T script -C none -n "postmarketOS boot script" -d /tmp/boot.txt /mnt/emmcboot/boot.scr`). 3. After booting again, verify `cat /proc/cmdline` shows `pmos_root=/dev/mmcblk2p2`. That way you don’t have to keep editing `/etc/fstab`, and the kernel will actually mount the EMMC root instead of falling back to the SD card UUID that was left over. Let me know if you’d like me to help craft the replacement script or test the rebuilt `boot.scr`.

ok explain this further. i can see that we are determining pmos_boot and pmos_root from a 'bootdev' environment variable which is controlled by mmc_bootdev. not sure how mmc_bootdev is itself set though.

Thinking:

Right now the script in `boot.scr` is doing exactly what you described: it reads `mmc_bootdev` and, based on that, sets `bootdev` to either `0` (SD) or `2` (eMMC) before building `bootargs`. That’s how `pmos_boot=/dev/mmcblk${bootdev}p1` and `pmos_root=/dev/mmcblk${bootdev}p2` end up pointing to `/dev/mmcblk0*` whenever `mmc_bootdev=0`, and to `/dev/mmcblk2*` when `mmc_bootdev≠0`. `mmc_bootdev` itself isn’t something we wrote into the script—it’s set much earlier, by U-Boot’s SPL (the “secondary program loader”). The SPL knows which MMC controller it loaded from: when the SoC boots from the SD slot it loads from MMC0 and exports `mmc_bootdev=0`; when it boots from the internal eMMC it loads from MMC2 and exports `mmc_bootdev=2`. The boot.scr script just uses that information so it can re-use the detected device for both `/boot` and `/`. If you boot with the SD card removed, the SPL should set `mmc_bootdev=2` and the script will self-adjust; that’s why the logs you eventually get from a clean EMMC boot should show `/dev/mmcblk2*` being mounted. If the SPL is still reporting `mmc_bootdev=0` even though you’re (supposed to be) booting from eMMC, then we can either: 1. Force the boot script to ignore `mmc_bootdev` and hard-code `bootdev=2` (e.g., edit the script before `setenv bootargs`), or 2. Permanently set the environment variable to 2 via `fw_setenv mmc_bootdev 2`. For option 2 you need a working `/etc/fw_env.config` that points at the U-Boot environment area on the eMMC; once that file exists you can run `fw_printenv mmc_bootdev` and `fw_setenv mmc_bootdev 2` to flip it. Otherwise, editing `boot.scr` to force `bootdev=2` (and re-generating the script with `mkimage`) is the quickest way to guarantee the rest of the boot args use `/dev/mmcblk2`. Let me know if you’d like me to help with either approach (editing the script or creating an `fw_env.config` so we can adjust the environment).

Thinking:

You’re asking two very reasonable questions: 1. **How do we know what `mmc_bootdev` is doing?** In your current SSH session we’re still booted off the SD card (the `lsblk` output shows `/dev/mmcblk0p2` as `/`, and `cat /proc/cmdline` shows `pmos_boot=/dev/mmcblk0p1`/`pmos_root=/dev/mmcblk0p2`). That means the SPL/U-Boot path already set `mmc_bootdev=0`, and the script honorably built `pmos_*` out of `/dev/mmcblk0…`. So nothing is “wrong” with `mmc_bootdev` yet—this is just the result of still booting from the SD card. When you finally boot with *only* the eMMC inserted, SPL should identify MMC2 as the boot source, set `mmc_bootdev=2`, and the script will automatically switch to `/dev/mmcblk2*`. If you want to verify the value at runtime, boot from eMMC, then run `fw_printenv mmc_bootdev` (after ensuring `/etc/fw_env.config` points at the eMMC environment) or just check `/proc/cmdline` again; if it still says `pmos_root=/dev/mmcblk0p2` then the bootloader is still seeing “boot from MMC0.” 2. **What does `/boot` actually do once the system is up?** The files in `/boot` (kernel image, `initramfs`, Device Tree blobs, `boot.scr`) are what U-Boot loads before it hands control to the kernel. Once the kernel is running, the files inside `/boot` aren’t read again during normal operation—they’re “there for show” from the running system’s perspective but are still required for kernel updates, regenerating `boot.scr`, etc. Mounting `/boot` via `fstab` just makes those files available so you can update kernels, DTBs, or boot scripts without chrooting elsewhere. In other words: `/boot` is essential at boot time, but once userspace is up, it mostly just sits there waiting for maintenance or updates. So, in summary: the “mount mmcblk0” entries you see in the log are from the SD-root boot you’re still in, not from the EMMC setup. Once you boot from eMMC alone, `mmc_bootdev` will flip to 2 automatically and `/boot` will be `/dev/mmcblk2p1`. Nothing in `/mnt/emmcroot/var/log` will reflect that until you actually do that boot (at which point you’ll get a fresh `dmesg` showing mmcblk2 entries).

Right—this is where the layers overlap. The running system you’re connected to is still booted from the SD card (that’s why `lsblk` shows `/dev/mmcblk0p2` mounted as `/` and `/proc/cmdline` still references `pmos_root=/dev/mmcblk0p2`), but the `/etc/fstab` you’re now viewing is the copy on that SD-root system and, during our edits, we already replaced the `/boot` and `/` entries there with the EMMC UUIDs (`41c1be64…` for `/boot`, `c880538a…` for `/`). That means, even while the system is running off the SD card, it’s *trying* to mount the EMMC partitions wherever they exist (e.g., `/boot` is now mounted from `/dev/mmcblk2p1` as soon as you boot). So it works because kernel/root were still the SD card’s copy, but fstab happily mounts the EMMC versions of `/boot` (and, if present, `/`). It’s not a contradiction—it’s just the fact that the startup path (kernel cmdline and initramfs) still points to MMC0 until you actually reboot with only MMC2 present. Once you do, both the bootloader (via `mmc_bootdev`) and `/etc/fstab` will be aligned to the EMMC partitions, and you’ll be running entirely from mmcblk2.

Thinking:

Right, and that’s expected while you’re still booted from the SD card: the kernel command line (from the SD-root initramfs) still sets `pmos_root=/dev/mmcblk0p2`, so `/` stays on mmcblk0 even though `/boot` is now mounted from mmcblk2p1 because `fstab` points there. That’s how you can have a hybrid state during these experiments. The moment you boot with only the eMMC present, SPL will identify MMC2 as the boot source, `mmc_bootdev` will become `2`, `boot.scr` will build `pmos_root=/dev/mmcblk2p2`, and `/` will finally mount from the EMMC root. Until you do that, the running kernel keeps using the SD root even though `/boot` is mounted from mmcblk2p1. So nothing is broken yet—it just means we still need to power-cycle with the SD card removed to confirm the EMMC root actually works.

what's the relation between fstab and the kernel command line? i thought that fstab basically gives a list of mounts that the system needs to perform. sounds like the system _is_ performing the one mount (for mmcblk2p1) but does not perform the other one (for mmcblk2p2)

Thinking:

Exactly: the kernel command line decides the *root filesystem* at the very beginning of the boot, before `init` or `systemd` ever runs `fstab`. That’s why you still see `/` coming from `mmcblk0p2` on the running SD-root machine—`pmos_root=/dev/mmcblk0p2` was baked into the kernel args that the bootloader passed. `/etc/fstab` comes later. Once the init system starts, it reads `fstab` and mounts everything listed there except the root (since it’s already mounted). That’s why you see `/boot` being mounted from `mmcblk2p1`: the kernel booted from the SD root but, because we rewrote `fstab`, it happily mounted `/boot` from the EMMC partition. `/etc/fstab` doesn’t remount `/`: the root filesystem was already chosen by the kernel and stays where it started. So during your testing you’re in a mixed state: root is still the SD card because the kernel command line says so, but `/boot` is the EMMC device because that’s what `fstab` now states. When you finally boot with only the eMMC inserted, the bootloader (via `mmc_bootdev`) will set the kernel command line to point to `mmcblk2p2`, and at that point both the kernel root and the `fstab` mounts will agree on the EMMC device.
ok. so why do we even list the root partition in fstab in the first place? just for our own sanity?

Thinking:

Exactly—listing `/` in `/etc/fstab` isn’t strictly required for mount mechanics (the kernel already mounted it) but it isn’t just “for show,” either. The root entry lets the init system: - Remount `/` with the desired mount options (e.g., `rw`, `nodev`, `relatime`, etc.) after the kernel initially mounts it. - Drive `fsck` behavior via the sixth column, so service scripts know whether to check root before boot. - Keep a canonical record of “what should be mounted where,” which helps tooling like `mount -a`, recovery shells, and emergency scripts when they need to remount or validate the root filesystem. So while the kernel command line decides which block device becomes `/`, `/etc/fstab` still captures the canonical configuration, remount-time tweaks, and policies that matter for the rest of the boot process.

Thinking:

Yes—this discussion actually gives us solid debugging cues: 1. **Kernel command line vs. `fstab`**: The running system still boots from `/dev/mmcblk0p2` because the kernel args (set by `mmc_bootdev`) point there. `/etc/fstab` can only remount `/` later, so having the EMMC root entry there doesn’t change the device the kernel already chose. That’s why you now see `/boot` mounted from mmcblk2p1 (because we rewrote `fstab`) while `/` remains `/dev/mmcblk0p2`. The takeaway is that we can’t rely on `fstab` alone to switch to EMMC; we must ensure the bootloader/kernel command line also targets mmcblk2. 2. **`mmc_bootdev` handling**: `mmc_bootdev` comes from the SoC/U-Boot SPL and tells the boot script whether to use `/dev/mmcblk0` or `/dev/mmcblk2`. When you remove the SD card, SPL should set it to `2` and the script will automatically build `pmos_root=/dev/mmcblk2p2`. If that isn’t happening, we’ll see it because `/proc/cmdline` will still mention `mmcblk0` even after an EMMC-only boot. If needed we can either update the U-Boot environment (`fw_setenv mmc_bootdev 2`) or rewrite the script so it hardcodes mmcblk2. 3. **Logs are already on the EMMC root**: When you do boot from mmcblk2, `/mnt/emmcroot/var/log` will contain the new logs, so truncating/clearing them now was the right move to get a clean picture of that boot. Next steps: power-cycle the phone with only the EMMC inserted, then immediately capture `/mnt/emmcroot/var/log/dmesg` (and any other logs) to see what stage it hangs at. If `/proc/cmdline` still shows `pmos_root=/dev/mmcblk0p2`, we’ll know SPL/U-Boot never switched to mmcblk2 and can either use `fw_setenv` or update `boot.scr` to force `bootdev=2`. When you boot with the SD card, `cat /proc/cmdline` reads `pmos_boot=/dev/mmcblk0p1 pmos_root=/dev/mmcblk0p2`, which is exactly what the `boot.scr` script emits when `bootdev` is `0`. Once you boot from EMMC only, that same file should show `pmos_boot=/dev/mmcblk2p1 pmos_root=/dev/mmcblk2p2`, proving that the SPL/U-Boot environment set `mmc_bootdev=2`. If it doesn’t, `mmc_bootdev` never flipped and we have to either adjust the U-Boot env (`fw_setenv mmc_bootdev 2` via an `fw_env.config` pointing at the eMMC environment) or hard-code `bootdev=2` in the script and rebuild `boot.scr` so the kernel args always point at mmcblk2. Next steps: 1. Reboot the phone with the SD card removed so the bootloader actually runs from eMMC and sets `mmc_bootdev=2`. 2. As soon as it hangs, remount `/mnt/emmcroot` from your host and read `/mnt/emmcroot/var/log/dmesg` plus any other newly populated logs. Thinking:

The “allwinner” directory you saw under `/mnt/emmcboot/` is just part of the stock boot tree on the PinePhone. Inside it there’s an `emmcboot` subdir that mirrors the top-level boot partition layout and an `emmcroot` subdir that mirrors the root filesystem—those are there for image-building/descriptive purposes rather than being actively mounted. PostmarketOS ships those so you can see, for example, “what would be written to eMMC” versus “what is currently on this boot media,” and they’re also useful for installers that copy from one layout into another. So when you `ls /mnt/emmcboot/allwinner`, you’re seeing a packaged copy of: - `appstream-data/` (metadata for app installers), - `emmcboot/` (a duplicate of the boot contents), - `emmcroot/` (a full tree of the intended root filesystem). They’re not special mountpoints themselves, just static files that live on the boot partition, and they don’t affect the actual `/boot` or `/` mounts.

Those `allwinner/emmcboot` and `allwinner/emmcroot` directories are just staging artifacts that postmarketOS drops onto the boot partition when it builds the image. what are the .dtb files?

Thinking:

`.dtb` stands for **Device Tree Blob**—it’s a compiled binary representation of a device tree source (`.dts`) that describes the hardware layout (CPU, memory, peripherals, buses, GPIO mappings, clocks, etc.) to the Linux kernel at boot time. On ARM platforms like the PinePhone the kernel doesn’t probe hardware dynamically the way x86 does; instead it relies on the DTB to know what devices exist and how they’re wired. So: - `sun50i-a64-pinephone-1.1.dtb` / `1.2` are the hardware descriptions for different PinePhone revisions. - `sun50i-a64-psci.dtbo` is a **Device Tree Overlay**—it’s applied on top of the base DTB to describe optional features (like PSCI idle states). - The bootloader loads these `.dtb` files into memory before jumping to the kernel, so the kernel sees a tailored view of the PinePhone’s hardware. If you ever need to tweak GPIOs or enable/disable peripherals, you edit the `.dts` and recompile to `.dtb`, but the binaries themselves live in `/boot` and are used every time you boot.

Thinking:

`init.sh` actually lives inside the EMMC root FS, not on the boot partition. The `boot.scr` script is loading `/boot/initramfs` and the kernel then unpacks that initramfs, which contains `/usr/share/initramfs/init.sh` (and `usr/libexec/rc/sh/init.sh`)—that’s the “init” that gets executed before the real root is pivoted in. When `boot.scr` sets `init=/init.sh`, it’s telling the kernel to start with the script baked into the initramfs. You can see the files yourself on the mounted root: ``` sudo find /mnt/emmcroot -name init.sh -print /mnt/emmcroot/usr/libexec/rc/sh/init.sh /mnt/emmcroot/usr/share/initramfs/init.sh ``` So `init.sh` isn’t missing—it’s inside the root filesystem and/or inside the initramfs that the kernel loads from `/boot`.

That works once the SPL/U-Boot path lands on eMMC, but while we’re still debugging we can temporarily override it to prove the kernel+rootfs on mmcblk2 work. Here’s what to do: 1. **Edit the boot script source**: create a temporary `boot.txt` (you can base it on the printed script you already saw) and change the `setenv bootargs` line to hard-code the EMMC devices. For example: ``` setenv bootdev 2 setenv bootargs init=/bin/sh rw console=tty0 ... pmos_boot=/dev/mmcblk2p1 pmos_root=/dev/mmcblk2p2 ``` Or append `init=/bin/sh` to drop to a shell instead of running `init.sh`. 2. **Rebuild `/boot/boot.scr`**: ```bash mkimage -A arm -O linux -T script -C none -n "postmarketOS boot script" -d boot.txt /mnt/emmcboot/boot.scr ``` 3. **Reboot with only the eMMC**. This will now boot with the modified args (no dependence on `mmc_bootdev`), so you can confirm the kernel starts, drops into the shell, and mounts `/` from `mmcblk2p2` (check `/proc/cmdline` once it’s running). 4. Once you’ve proven that, restore the original `boot.scr` or reintroduce the dynamic logic but force `bootdev=2`. That lets you revert to normal boot once the underlying problem (whatever hung in init/SXMO) is isolated. Let me know when you’re ready and I can help write the temporary `boot.txt` and the `mkimage` command, or we can instead install a minimal `init=/bin/sh` override directly in the current script.
